questionText: Prove that the sum of any five consecutive integers is divisible by $5$.

steps.explanation: Centring the five consecutive integers on the middle value $n$ makes the outer terms cancel in pairs when we add.
We add all five integers, keeping everything in terms of the middle value $n$.
The $-2$ and $+2$ cancel, and the $-1$ and $+1$ cancel, leaving five copies of $n$.
Writing the sum as $5$ times $n$ exposes the factor of $5$ we are trying to prove is present.
The middle value $n$ is an integer, so $S$ is $5$ multiplied by a whole number.
A number equal to $5$ times an integer is a multiple of $5$ by definition.
The sum of any five consecutive integers equals $5n$, so it is always divisible by $5$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Centring on $n$, the sum is $(n-2)+(n-1)+n+(n+1)+(n+2)=5n$, a multiple of $5$. Hence the sum of any five consecutive integers is divisible by $5$. $\blacksquare$
